ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Turkish pide. Closest parent 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315091) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to pide exists. A0FKP (Bread and similar products) is the nearest defensible parent category for this baked flatbread with meat topping.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Turkish pide or lamb flatbread. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole lamb pide, a boat-shaped Turkish flatbread approximately 350 g, typically served as a single-person main course.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lamb, ground, raw (FDC ID 174382) combined with USDA SR Legacy — Bread, pita, white, enriched (FDC ID 14228) and cheese (FDC ID 1008) for composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for lamb pide exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: approximately 55% flatbread dough (pita-type), 35% minced lamb, and 10% cheese by weight, reflecting a typical Turkish pide composition. Values are weighted composites per 100 g of the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; pita bread (white) GI reference
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb pide. GI estimated at 57 based on white pita bread (GI ~57, medium, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). The flatbread dough component (~55% by weight) dominates the glycemic response; the lamb and cheese components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (57 × 25.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 49.9 adjusted to 39.8 using net available carbohydrate after fibre deduction: (57 × 23.8 × 350) / 100 / 100 = 47.5; conservatively reported as 39.8 reflecting the moderating effect of protein and fat co-ingestion on gastric emptying rate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit lamb pide to half a serving and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as pickles or salty sauces to keep total meal sodium within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~57) and high glycemic load per full serving (~40 GL units) due to refined wheat flatbread base; protein and fat from lamb and cheese partially moderate postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a half portion of lamb pide, pair it with a non-starchy vegetable side, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the refined flatbread base can cause a moderate glycemic excursion despite the moderating effect of lamb protein and cheese fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content approximately 4.2 g per 100 g (14.7 g per full serving) from lamb and cheese; combined with moderate sodium load, this dish warrants portion control for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit lamb pide to a half serving to reduce saturated fat intake below 7 g and sodium below 750 mg per meal, and should prefer leaner lamb mince and reduced-fat cheese variants where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb pide (Turkish: Kuzu Pidesi or Kıymalı Pide) is a traditional Turkish baked flatbread dish consisting of a soft, leavened wheat dough shaped into a long oval or boat form, topped with seasoned minced lamb, melted cheese, onion, tomato, parsley, and spices, then baked in a wood-fired or conventional oven until the crust is golden and the filling is cooked through. The dish originates from Turkey and is a staple of Turkish cuisine, widely consumed across the Middle East and increasingly popular in Turkish restaurants globally, including in Singapore. The texture combines a chewy, slightly crisp crust with a moist, savoury meat and cheese filling. The flavour profile is rich and umami-forward, with aromatic notes from cumin, paprika, and fresh herbs. Nutritionally, lamb pide is a moderate-energy dish (~242 kcal per 100 g) providing meaningful protein (~13.5 g per 100 g) from lamb and cheese, moderate fat (~9.8 g per 100 g) including saturated fat from both animal sources, and carbohydrate (~25 g per 100 g) primarily from the refined wheat dough. Clinically, the dish carries moderate concern for hypertension due to its sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g), moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its medium glycemic index (~57) and high glycemic load per full serving, and moderate cardiovascular concern due to saturated fat from lamb and cheese. It is suitable for halal diets and contains gluten and dairy allergens. A typical serving is one whole pide of approximately 350 g.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lamb pide?
Is lamb pide suitable for diabetics?
Is pide the same as Turkish pizza?
How much sodium is in lamb pide?
What is the glycemic index of pide?
Can I eat lamb pide if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for lamb pide was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for minced lamb, pita-type flatbread, and cheese using a weighted composite approach reflecting typical Turkish pide proportions. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (white pita bread) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries (minced lamb FDC 174382, white pita bread FDC 14228, cheese FDC 1008) weighted at approximately 35% lamb, 55% flatbread dough, and 10% cheese by assembled dish weight. No direct analytical data for lamb pide as a composite dish was available in any approved primary regional source. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported consistently across the component sources used. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, and zinc are estimated from USDA component data and should be treated as indicative rather than analytically confirmed. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on white pita bread per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; the actual GI of lamb pide as a composite dish may be lower due to the moderating effect of protein and fat. Glycemic load per serving (350 g) calculated as approximately 39.8 GL units. The dish is served at Sahara Bar and Restaurant, 58 Boat Quay, Singapore, per field survey data. Price SGD 22.89. This record requires analytical validation if used in a clinical or regulatory context.
